one_line_takeaway: A Perplexity-authored NIST/CAISI RFI response arguing that agent security requires a three-layer defense-in-depth stack -- input filtering, model-level instruction hierarchies, and above all a deterministic (non-LLM) enforcement layer -- because probabilistic defenses alone cannot give hard guarantees against indirect prompt injection and confused-deputy risks in multi-agent systems.
key_finding: The paper's central normative claim is that no single defense layer is sufficient: input-level detectors suffer badly from the base-rate fallacy in high-volume, low-attack-prevalence agentic traffic (most flagged 'attacks' become false alarms) and add latency across multi-step workflows; model-level instruction-hierarchy training is architecturally not a hard guarantee because roles are flattened into one token sequence with no enforcement mechanism, and autoregressive recency/RLHF-compliance bias tends to make models favor the most recent (often lower-priority) instruction; therefore a deterministic, LLM-independent enforcement layer (tool allowlists, rate limits on sensitive operations, schema/regex validation before execution) is argued to be the only layer offering hard protection guarantees, and of the three layers it is characterized as the most mature/widely deployed in production today, while input-level and model-level defenses remain research-stage with only early production deployment.
core_contribution: This is Perplexity AI's (Ninghui Li, Kaiyuan Zhang, Kyle Polley, Jerry Ma; Perplexity + Purdue) formal written response to a NIST/CAISI Request for Information (RFI 2025-0035) on AI agent security, lightly adapted into an arXiv article. It is a position/policy paper, not an empirical study: it (1) argues that LLM-powered agents represent a new, more severe instance of the recurring 'code-data separation' problem in computing history (von Neumann architecture -> web injection -> mobile app sandboxing -> agent systems), (2) organizes agent security risks using the CIA triad (confidentiality/integrity/availability) crossed with five adversary classes (external content providers, component providers, network-based adversaries, insiders, client-side adversaries) and named attack surfaces (tool selection logic, tool execution boundaries, web-grounded ingestion, multi-agent coordination surfaces, skill/plugin supply chains), (3) analyzes multi-agent-specific risks -- implicit delegation, confused-deputy attacks between an outer/less-privileged agent and an inner/more-privileged agent, and privilege escalation through agent chains, (4) proposes a three-layer defense-in-depth stack (input-level detection, model-level instruction-hierarchy/embedding defenses, and a 'deterministic last line of defense' using conventional verifiable code such as tool allowlists/blocklists, rate limits, and schema validation), and (5) recommends research directions to NIST/CAISI: adaptive (not static) security benchmarks, RBAC-plus-risk-adaptive-access-control hybrid models for agent authorization, and human-factors research on confirmation fatigue / risk-aware autonomy.
threat_model: Adversaries who can inject content into channels an agent ingests during normal operation (e.g., poisoned web pages, emails, calendar entries -- indirect prompt injection), plus four other adversary classes the paper explicitly enumerates: component providers (malicious/buggy LLM services, memory services, tools, skills), network-based adversaries (targeting API endpoints, webhooks, inter-component channels), insiders (developers/operators introducing vulnerabilities via bugs or misconfiguration), and client-side adversaries (with access to the user's client device). In multi-agent settings the threat model extends to a less-privileged 'outer' agent being manipulated into inducing a more-privileged 'inner' agent to act (confused deputy), and to non-adversarial system failures (model errors, stale-state replay, bad memory updates) that can also produce CIA violations without any attacker.
limitations: The paper is explicitly a position/opinion piece submitted to a regulatory RFI, not a research paper with new experiments -- it presents no original dataset, no benchmark results, no quantitative evaluation of the defenses it discusses, and no baselines/ablations of its own. Claims about defense maturity and attack surfaces are asserted from operational experience and secondary citations rather than measured. Several sections reference the authors' own separate prior work (AgentSandbox [53], BrowseSafe [54]) without reproducing quantitative results from those papers in this document, so those results cannot be verified from this text alone. It also acknowledges an open problem it does not solve: fully specifying legitimate control flows for control/data-flow-separation frameworks like CaMeL remains unresolved for open-ended agent systems.
